title: The Cohesion Policy Paradox: Closing the Gap Between Countries, Not Within Them
content:
Negotiations over the EU’s Multiannual Financial Framework (MFF) have traditionally pitted net contributors against net beneficiaries. At the heart of the dispute lies a fundamental disagreement over the purpose of the EU budget.

The largest spending programmes have long been agricultural subsidies and cohesion policy. The willingness of so called frugal member states to continue financing these programmes depends largely on whether they deliver tangible results and contribute to broader European priorities.

The primary objective of EU cohesion policy is to reduce economic, social and territorial disparities between regions across the Union – a goal enshrined in the EU treaties themselves. As a result, the main beneficiaries are so-called cohesion countries, whose gross national income (GNI) per capita remains below 90% of the EU average.

Most of these countries joined the EU in 2004, and accession marked the beginning of a period of rapid economic growth. Poland’s economic convergence with Western Europe is among the EU’s most notable success stories. Its GDP per capita rose from roughly 51% of the EU average in 2004 to around 80–81% in 2024–2025.
snippet: Negotiations over the EU’s Multiannual Financial Framework (MFF) have traditionally pitted net contributors against net beneficiaries. At the heart of the dispute lies a fundamental disagreement over
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Referendums, the West and Putin’s anger. What awaits Armenia after the election?
content:
However, the margin was not large enough to give him a free hand to implement all the reforms needed to keep Armenia firmly on its Western trajectory. Meanwhile, Russia is likely to continue rattling its sabre and looking for ways to undermine its “younger brothers” in the South Caucasus, though outright military action remains unlikely, foreign policy analysts told Delfi when asked to assess the situation in Armenia following the election.

As previously reported, Armenia has in recent years reoriented its foreign policy away from Russia and towards the West. In the parliamentary election held recently, voters reaffirmed that course, handing a convincing victory to Pashinyan’s Civil Contract party. It won 49.8% of the vote, while the largest opposition force, the pro-Russian Strong Armenia party, received 23.3%. The centrist-left Armenia Alliance entered parliament with 9.9%, while the conservative Prosperous Armenia secured 4%.
snippet: However, the margin was not large enough to give him a free hand to implement all the reforms needed to keep Armenia firmly on its Western trajectory. Meanwhile, Russia is likely to continue rattling
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Drones are transforming defence, but European countries are struggling to keep up
content:
Countries must be capable not only of acquiring modern equipment, but also of creating an environment in which it can be rapidly deployed for practical use.

Currently, however, this process is still lagging behind. Participants at the Drone Summit 2026 in Riga agreed that countries must work together to accelerate the development and deployment of drone and counter-drone technologies, as well as other innovations.

Whereas only a few years ago defence experts discussed future warfare largely in theoretical terms, many technologies are now evolving under conditions of real and immediate threat. According to experts, drones have become one of the most significant elements on today’s battlefield — serving both as a valuable asset and a serious threat.

At the same time, several obstacles continue to hinder faster progress in the sector. Experts highlighted a fragmented market, cumbersome procurement procedures and limited access to financing as major challenges.
snippet: Countries must be capable not only of acquiring modern equipment, but also of creating an environment in which it can be rapidly deployed for practical use.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How We Created the Deadly Heat
content:
This was the 125th study conducted under the World Weather Attribution initiative, an international scientific programme launched eleven years ago with a single, clearly defined mission: to provide real-time scientific evidence of the role human-induced climate change plays in extreme weather events. When temperatures across Western, Central and Northern Europe shattered every known record in late June 2025, the researchers did not wait for the season to end. They got to work immediately.

Their findings leave no room for interpretation. The extreme heat that disrupted schools, hospitals, transport networks and workplaces across Europe would have been impossible just half a century ago. The climate Europe knew in 1976 has changed irreversibly.

Professor Friederike Otto, one of the project’s founders, was unequivocal:

“Human-induced climate change – which we know with certainty from decades of research and multiple independent lines of evidence – is caused by the burning of fossil fuels: coal, oil and gas, and to a much lesser extent by deforestation.”
snippet: This was the 125th study conducted under the World Weather Attribution initiative, an international scientific programme launched eleven years ago with a single, clearly defined mission: to provide
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Poland’s LNG Hub Ambitions Face New Scrutiny
content:
However, the decision comes at a particularly challenging moment: following the disruption of part of Qatar’s gas supplies, amid Europe’s record dependence on United States liquefied natural gas, and against forecasts of declining gas demand.

On Tuesday, 23 June, Energy Minister Miłosz Motyka announced on X that Poland would build its third liquefied natural gas terminal – the FSRU 2 floating regasification unit in the Gulf of Gdańsk. He accompanied the announcement with a statement that stood out even against the typically ambitious rhetoric surrounding energy security.

“The FSRU 2 terminal in the Gulf of Gdańsk will significantly expand our gas import capacity and strengthen Poland’s resilience to any disruptions on energy markets. We are consistently building a new architecture of Europe’s energy security. Poland is becoming a hub that will ensure stable gas supplies for the entire region,” the minister stated..
snippet: However, the decision comes at a particularly challenging moment: following the disruption of part of Qatar’s gas supplies, amid Europe’s record dependence on United States liquefied natural gas, and
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: No, Romania Has Not Started the Process of Annexing the Republic of Moldova
content:
The accusations resurfaced after, on Wednesday, June 24, a legislative proposal initiated by the SOS Romania parliamentary group regarding the unification of Romania and the Republic of Moldova was tacitly adopted by the Chamber of Deputies.

Tacit adoption means that a bill is approved without a plenary debate and vote after the legal deadline has expired.

The proposal will now move to the Senate, where its chances of being adopted are virtually nonexistent.

According to the bill, the Romanian Parliament “authorizes the Government to immediately begin urgent negotiations with the authorities in Chișinău in order to finalize the union with the Republic of Moldova.”

“Following the adoption of this law and its publication in the Official Gazette, the competent international authorities, the Government of the Republic of Moldova, the United States, NATO, the United Nations, and the European Union shall be notified for the implementation of the provisions of this law,” the draft legislation further states.

The explanatory memorandum claims that even the U.S. Congress supports such an initiative, citing a resolution adopted by the U.S. Senate on June 28, 1991.
snippet: The accusations resurfaced after, on Wednesday, June 24, a legislative proposal initiated by the SOS Romania parliamentary group regarding the unification of Romania and the Republic of Moldova was
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Moldova’s Fast Track to Europe
content:
European Council President António Costa, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and Moldovan President Maia Sandu presented a reform record that can hardly be described as anything other than a structural shift toward Europe — achieved despite Russia’s war against Ukraine, energy blackmail and unprecedented hybrid pressure from Moscow.

As Costa noted, the summit came at an “absolutely perfect moment.” Only a week earlier, the EU formally opened the first negotiation cluster, covering the rule of law, democratic institutions and fundamental rights. For a country that only a few years ago remained firmly within Russia’s sphere of influence and struggled with deeply entrenched corruption, the development marks a historic transformation.

Von der Leyen described the opening of the first negotiation cluster as a “historic milestone”, praising Moldova’s “unwavering determination, courage and hard work” in pursuing its European ambitions.

President Maia Sandu argued that Moldova now has a unique opportunity to secure its place in the European family.
snippet: European Council President António Costa, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and Moldovan President Maia Sandu presented a reform record that can hardly be described as anything other
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: URC in Gdańsk Without Zelenskyy? Poland Has Seen This Before
content:
Yet this is not the first time that a major international conference co-hosted by another country on Polish soil has become mired in controversy.

The Ukraine Recovery Conference was meant to reinforce Poland’s position as one of Ukraine’s closest allies and a key player in the debate over the country’s future reconstruction. Instead, preparations have been dominated by behind-the-scenes political tensions, symbolised above all by the absence of the event’s most important guest.

President Volodymyr Zelensky’s decision not to attend has cast a shadow over what was intended to be a diplomatic and political success story. The contracts and investment agreements expected to emerge from the conference will most likely still be signed. For now, however, much of the international media coverage has focused less on reconstruction plans and more on the growing dispute between Poland and Ukraine.

It was confirmed on Monday (22.06) that Volodymyr Zelenskyy will not attend the Ukraine Recovery Conference (URC) in Gdańsk, with the Ukrainian delegation instead set to be led by Prime Minister Yulia Svyrydenko.
snippet: Yet this is not the first time that a major international conference co-hosted by another country on Polish soil has become mired in controversy.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Ukraine and the EU: When Could Membership Happen, What Are the Obstacles, and What Would It Mean for Europe?
content:
Ukraine still faces major hurdles ranging from corruption and agricultural reform to minority rights. And while President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has spoken of joining the EU by 2028, experts and European partners remain far more cautious.

The debate over Ukraine’s European future dates back to 2014, when demonstrators waving EU flags on Kyiv’s Maidan Square succeeded in forcing then-president Viktor Yanukovych from power after he abandoned plans to sign an association agreement with the EU.

For years, little concrete progress followed. The turning point came in 2022 with Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Faced with the geopolitical implications of the war, EU member states concluded that the question of Ukrainian membership could no longer be postponed.

Ukraine formally submitted its application to join the bloc in 2022. The move was widely welcomed across Europe, with one notable exception: Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, who repeatedly blocked progress.

That obstacle disappeared in May this year when Orbán was replaced by Péter Magyar. His government lifted Hungary’s objections, allowing the EU to formally open accession negotiations in June.
snippet: Ukraine still faces major hurdles ranging from corruption and agricultural reform to minority rights. And while President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has spoken of joining the EU by 2028, experts and European
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Veștea Government Rejected by Parliament Amid Controversial Talks with Romania`s Far Right
content:
After repeatedly declaring that cooperation with AUR was a red line, President Nicușor Dan stood by as his own nominee for prime minister negotiated with the party led by George Simion. “We are witnessing the banality of evil,” commented Kelemen Hunor, leader of the Democratic Alliance of Hungarians in Romania (UDMR).

Romania has been without a government since May 5, when a no-confidence motion tabled by the far-right AUR and supported by the Social Democratic Party, then a member of the governing coalition, was approved by Parliament.

After parliamentary parties failed to produce a viable solution during consultations with the president, Nicușor Dan nominated Eugen Tomac, a Member of the European Parliament from the PMP/Renew group and one of his close advisers, to form a government.

Tomac was unable to convince parties to support his cabinet and resigned on June 14. On the same day, President Dan announced that he would nominate Liberal politician Adrian Veștea to form a government.
snippet: After repeatedly declaring that cooperation with AUR was a red line, President Nicușor Dan stood by as his own nominee for prime minister negotiated with the party led by George Simion. “We are
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Pornography, alcohol and endless scrolling: What are Polish children doing online?
content:
While most adults are still making their morning coffee, smartphone screens are already lighting up children’s bedrooms across Poland. Between 6 a.m. and 8 a.m., more than half (56%) of Polish children unlock their phones. One in three immediately dives into TikTok, while a similar share opens Messenger. And that is only the beginning of a typical day that, for the average user aged between 7 and 14, ends with a striking figure: 4 hours and 25 minutes spent online.

No other age group—not even adults—spends more time connected to the internet.

The newly released report Internet Children 2026, prepared by the Institute of Digital Citizenship, paints a stark picture of the digital reality facing the youngest generation. This year’s edition moves beyond simple screen-time statistics and app rankings. Instead, drawing on hard behavioural data rather than self-reported user declarations, researchers examined the entire architecture of children’s digital environment.
snippet: While most adults are still making their morning coffee, smartphone screens are already lighting up children’s bedrooms across Poland. Between 6 a.m. and 8 a.m., more than half (56%) of Polish
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Hegseth Announces Review of U.S. Force Posture in Europe
content:
Opening his remarks, Pete Hegseth reiterated the position of the Trump administration, which he said has spent the past eighteen months pressing allies to assume greater responsibility for their own defence.

“President Trump has made it clear over many years and across two administrations that NATO cannot remain a paper tiger or a one-way street. That era is over,” he said.

Hegseth challenged the foundations of the post-Cold War European security order, arguing that NATO was never intended to function as a system in which the burden of defending Europe rested primarily on the United States. He invoked the words of former U.S. President and Supreme Allied Commander Dwight D. Eisenhower, who warned in 1951 that a permanent American military presence in Europe would signal a failure of the Alliance’s original purpose.

“If American troops stationed in Europe have not returned home within ten years, the entire project will have failed,” Hegseth quoted Dwight Eisenhower as saying.
snippet: Opening his remarks, Pete Hegseth reiterated the position of the Trump administration, which he said has spent the past eighteen months pressing allies to assume greater responsibility for their own
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: From Political Crisis to Emergency Drills: Inside Latvia’s Race to Secure Its Skies
content:
Emergency sirens wailed across Latvia alongside the rollout of cell broadcast alerts. Concurrently, residents received smartphone notifications explaining the nature of the drill.

During the test, a fluctuating audio signal sounded for three minutes — consisting of a five-second blast followed by a brief pause, repeating throughout the window. The exercise aimed to identify potential technical faults or disruptions in the siren infrastructure and evaluate overall signal audibility.

“Latvia has been testing its warning systems regularly for many years — usually twice a year,” Sandra Užule-Fons, a Latvian-born journalist, historian, and documentary filmmaker specialising in the Baltic region, told FocusEurope.pl.

She notes that these nationwide drills took place in 2022, 2023, and 2024 as routine checks of system readiness. However, she points out that the inclusion of cell broadcast text alerts — introduced in Latvia in the latter half of last year — is a new development.
snippet: Emergency sirens wailed across Latvia alongside the rollout of cell broadcast alerts. Concurrently, residents received smartphone notifications explaining the nature of the drill.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Studies find no cardiovascular effects from wind turbine infrasound
content:
In early summer, opponents of wind power have been circulating a claim on Finnish-language social media that infrasound from wind turbines poses a risk to cardiovascular health. 

Infrasound is very low-frequency sound (below 20 Hz) that humans can only hear when it is loud enough – over 90–110 decibels (dB). The infrasound produced by wind turbines generally remains below this level at a distance of 100–200 metres.

As well as wind turbines, infrasound is also produced by, for example, ocean waves, the wind and the rumble of thunder, as well as washing machines, trains, aeroplanes and so on. According to researchers, even walking and moving one’s head up and down cause fluctuations in air pressure in the ears that are equivalent to infrasound of over 75 dB. 

The debate has been brought to the fore by a recent study published in Germany, which claims that there are “significantly increased incidence of heart failure and cardiac arrhythmias in municipalities with substantial wind energy expansion”. In the publication, this claim was linked to low-frequency sound and infrasound.
snippet: In early summer, opponents of wind power have been circulating a claim on Finnish-language social media that infrasound from wind turbines poses a risk to cardiovascular health.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Earthquake in Hungarian media: public service broadcasting will not be one-party, the Fidesz empire is falling apart
content:
Although Péter Magyar promised during the campaign that one of his first measures after an election victory would be to switch off the public media “factory of lies”, this ultimately did not happen. Three days after the election, he appeared in the studio of Kossuth Radio and M1, where he got into a sharp exchange with the presenters.

“Regardless of this, what Tisza has undertaken will happen. After the formation of the government, one of the first steps will be to suspend the news service of this propaganda media,” he reiterated at the time. Tisza did not fulfill this promise in this form even after the government was formed.

The director-general of MTVA, the entity operating public service media, Dániel Papp, resigned in early June, citing the government’s preparations to reform public service broadcasting.

On 12 June, Tisza submitted amendments to the media law, which were approved by parliament on 23 June. The comprehensive amendment thoroughly restructures public service media, and the management will also be replaced as a result of this overhaul.
snippet: Although Péter Magyar promised during the campaign that one of his first measures after an election victory would be to switch off the public media “factory of lies”, this ultimately did not happen.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Has Latvia “challenged Brussels” on migration?
content:
The post presents this as Latvia “defying Brussels”. However, the claim is only partially accurate: it uses part of a genuine statement made by the minister but takes it out of context and exaggerates the nature of the EU migration mechanism.

In mid-May, a French-language post began circulating on the social media platform X, stating: “LATVIA CHALLENGES BRUSSELS! Foreign Minister Baiba Braže has just made a major announcement: ‘Latvia will NOT ACCEPT any new migrants under the EU pact and will NOT PAY €20,000 for every migrant demanded by Brussels!’ We are protecting our borders, not spending taxpayers’ money to finance imposed immigration. […]

Delfi contacted the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MFA) to verify whether Braže had indeed made such a statement. The ministry confirmed that the quotation was partially taken from Braže’s address during the Saeima’s foreign policy debate on the 29th of January. At the time, the minister stated: “As the Minister of the Interior has already emphasised, Latvia will not accept new migrants and will not pay for them. We will provide support and assistance to other countries.”
snippet: The post presents this as Latvia “defying Brussels”. However, the claim is only partially accurate: it uses part of a genuine statement made by the minister but takes it out of context and exaggerates
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: An image published by a politician exaggerates threats. Bartaševičs says he wanted to protect Children
content:
Following the alert issued on the 19th of May, when warnings were sent to residents across the widest area to date, public concern and tension were not eased a few days later by the image chosen for a Facebook post by long-time political heavyweight and former mayor of Latvia’s seventh-largest city, Rēzekne, Aleksandrs Bartaševičs (Together for Latvia).

Around midday on the 19th of May, concern spread among residents as warnings of a potential threat were issued shortly before noon in Ludza and Krāslava municipalities, and a short time later in Rēzekne and Preiļi municipalities.

The alert was subsequently extended to Madona, Cēsis, Gulbene and Smiltene municipalities, while after 1 p.m. a warning was also issued in Valmiera municipality, far from the Russian border. NATO fighter jets were deployed, the unified emergency services website 112.lv became unavailable, and a meeting of the Crisis Management Centre was convened. Afterwards, its head, Colonel Arvis Zīle, reassured the public that the suspected drone was no longer in Latvian airspace.
snippet: Following the alert issued on the 19th of May, when warnings were sent to residents across the widest area to date, public concern and tension were not eased a few days later by the image chosen for a
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: What Magyar’s first month as Hungary’s Prime Minister has looked like?
content:
He succeeded Viktor Orban, who had spent 16 years in power and was widely criticised during that time for moving closer to the Kremlin, drifting away from European values, the rule of law, media freedom and corruption problems.

Addressing the public after his Tisza party’s election victory in April, Peter Magyar pledged to do everything possible to usher in “a new era” for Hungary. Throughout the election campaign, he promised to tackle corruption, improve the country’s economic situation and secure the release of frozen EU funds, which had been withheld over concerns about the rule of law in Hungary. The promises were ambitious, but one month after taking office, can we already see the first steps towards fulfilling them?

One of the new government’s biggest achievements during its first month has been reaching an agreement with the European Commission (EC) on unlocking almost all of Hungary’s Recovery and Cohesion funds, provided the government implements a series of long-awaited reforms. Hungary must still fulfil a number of conditions before the funds are released in full.
snippet: He succeeded Viktor Orban, who had spent 16 years in power and was widely criticised during that time for moving closer to the Kremlin, drifting away from European values, the rule of law, media
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How EU Cohesion Funding Changed the Eastern Flank [INFOGRAPHIC]
content:
The experience of individual countries shows that the inflow of EU funding alone does not guarantee success – how the money is invested is just as important.

How much cohesion funding has the EU’s eastern flank received, and what impact has it had? The data in this infographic illustrate both the scale of EU support and how differently individual countries have made use of this historic opportunity.

The post How EU Cohesion Funding Changed the Eastern Flank [INFOGRAPHIC] appeared first on Eastflank.
snippet: The experience of individual countries shows that the inflow of EU funding alone does not guarantee success – how the money is invested is just as important.
creator: Zofia Grosse
